summary: Gruber details the evolution of iPhone calendar syncing from OS 1.0 to 2.0, explaining how MobileMe solved the major pain points of tethered-only syncing and poor multi-calendar support, but introduced frustrating new limitations. MobileMe syncing is all-or-nothing, preventing users from choosing which calendars sync, and it cannot sync read-only subscription calendars. He explores workarounds and finds none satisfactory. The piece concludes by lamenting that MobileMe has dismantled the 'digital hub' concept that iTunes once provided — a single place to manage all syncing — without offering any replacement for that control.
key_points: iPhone OS 2.0 and MobileMe fixed the two biggest calendar complaints — tethered-only syncing and lack of multi-calendar support — but introduced new regressions
MobileMe calendar syncing is all-or-nothing with no per-calendar control, meaning every alarm fires on every device simultaneously
Read-only subscription calendars (holidays, sports schedules) cannot sync to iPhone via MobileMe, whereas iTunes syncing supported them
The shift from iTunes to MobileMe as the syncing mechanism eliminated the 'digital hub' — a single place to manage what syncs where
Apple's slow feature cadence means fixes likely won't arrive until Snow Leopard
money_quotes: MobileMe syncing and iTunes syncing are mutually exclusive.
That's great. Too bad MobileMe introduces several new calendar syncing shortcomings.
A useful feature, gone, and now I'm badgered with reminders popping up in two places at once on my office desk.
No, syncing calendars and contacts and email accounts to an iPhone doesn't have anything to do with 'tunes'. But that which we call a rose by any other name would smell as sweet.
iTunes serves an essential role, that of the hub — one obvious place to go to manage everything that syncs to your iPod or iPhone.
But as it stands today, with MobileMe syncing, there is no hub.
One obvious solution would be an iCal update that lets you specify on a per-calendar basis which calendars should be synced to MobileMe.
key_insight: MobileMe solved iPhone calendar syncing's biggest problem — over-the-air sync — but in doing so destroyed the granular control and central management hub that iTunes provided, a classic case of fixing one thing by breaking another.
